section.title: As the Deer Pants for Water
section.sectionRationale: The opening psalm of Book II, Psalms 42-72, this is the first of the Korahite psalms and forms one continuous poem with Psalm 43, bound together by the same refrain repeated three times. It models the soul talking to itself rather than simply listening to itself in downcast despair, longing for God as a deer longs for water.
section.thesis: The psalmist's soul thirsts for the living God as a deer thirsts for water, and though downcast and taunted by enemies who ask 'Where is your God?', he preaches hope to his own soul, refusing despair and choosing to wait for God's salvation.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the soul's thirst for God amid tears and taunts, verses 1-4; the first refrain of self-address, remembering God even from the depths, verses 5-8; and the return of the taunt met again by the same refrain of hope, verses 9-11.

units.label: As the deer pants for streams of water
units.note: the soul's thirst amid tears and taunts

verses.text: As the deer {{arag|pants}} for streams of water, so my soul longs after You, O God.
My soul thirsts for God, the living God. When shall I come and appear in God's presence?
My tears have been my food both day and night, while men ask me all day long, "Where is your God?"
These things come to mind as I pour out my soul: how I walked with the multitude, leading the procession to the house of God with shouts of joy and praise.

units.label: Why are you downcast, O my soul

verses.text: Why are you {{shachach|downcast}}, O my soul? Why the unease within me? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise Him for the salvation of His presence.
O my God, my soul despairs within me. Therefore I remember You from the land of Jordan and the peaks of Hermon, even from Mount Mizar.
Deep calls to deep in the roar of Your waterfalls; all Your breakers and waves have rolled over me.

The LORD decrees His loving devotion by day, and at night His song is with me 
as a prayer to the God of my life.

units.label: I will yet praise Him, my Savior and my God
units.note: the taunt returns, and so does hope

verses.text: I say to God my Rock, "Why have You forgotten me? Why must I walk in sorrow because of the enemy's oppression?"
Like the crushing of my bones, my enemies taunt me, while they say to me all day long, "Where is your God?"
Why are you downcast, O my soul? Why the unease within me? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise Him, my Savior and my God.

arag.gloss: To pant or long intensely, used only here and in Joel 1:20 of animals crying out for water in drought, a physical, desperate longing rather than a mild wish, describing the soul's craving for God as bodily thirst.

shachach.gloss: Bowed down, downcast, or sunk low, from a root meaning to bow or crouch. The psalmist addresses his own soul directly by name in this state, preaching to it rather than merely listening to its despair.

basic.context: Likely written by someone exiled or far from the Jerusalem temple, remembering days of leading festival processions with shouts of joy, this psalmist now weeps day and night while enemies taunt him, 'Where is your God?' Rather than surrender to despair, he turns and speaks directly to his own soul, commanding it to hope in God, a refrain repeated at the end of each movement.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm models a crucial spiritual discipline: talking to oneself rather than simply listening to oneself. The psalmist does not deny his downcast, despairing feelings, but interrogates them and answers them with truth about God, showing that honest lament and disciplined hope are not opposites.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist's longing is arag, the intense panting of a thirst-stricken animal, used elsewhere only of creatures crying out in drought, Joel 1:20, picturing desperate physical need rather than casual desire. His soul is shachach, bowed down or sunk low, which he directly addresses and commands rather than simply obeying its despair.
basic.typology: The psalmist's soul crushed with sorrow while enemies taunt 'Where is your God?' anticipates Christ's own anguish, His soul 'deeply grieved, to the point of death,' Matthew 26:38, and the mockery He endured on the cross, 'He trusts in God; let God rescue Him now,' Matthew 27:43, a nearly identical taunt.
basic.christ: The taunt hurled at the psalmist, 'Where is your God?', anticipates the almost identical mockery hurled at Christ on the cross, 'He trusts in God; let God rescue Him now if He wants Him,' Matthew 27:43. Jesus Himself entered this psalm's very anguish, His soul deeply grieved even to death, Matthew 26:38, yet, like the psalmist, moved through that darkness toward resurrection praise, the pattern this psalm's refrain, 'I will yet praise Him,' anticipates and secures for all who are in Him.
basic.god: God is shown as the living water for which the soul thirsts, present even in the roar of overwhelming waves, His loving devotion decreed by day and His song given even in the darkest night.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 27:43 records mockers taunting Jesus on the cross with words strikingly similar to this psalm's 'Where is your God?'. Matthew 26:38 shows Jesus's own soul deeply grieved, echoing this psalm's downcast soul. John 7:37-38 pictures Jesus offering living water to all who thirst, answering this psalm's central longing.
basic.oneStory: The psalmist's thirsting soul, downcast yet preaching hope to itself amid taunts asking 'Where is your God?', anticipates Christ, who entered this very anguish on the cross, mocked with nearly identical words, yet who is Himself the living water for which every thirsty soul longs and the ground of the hope this psalm refuses to relinquish.

context.literary: The opening psalm of Book II, Psalms 42-72, and the first of the psalms attributed to the sons of Korah, forming one continuous poem with Psalm 43, bound together by the identical refrain repeated in verses 5, 11, and 43:5.
context.historical: Likely composed by a temple singer or Levite exiled or separated from Jerusalem, remembering with longing the festival processions he once led, now surrounded by enemies who mock his faith in his absence from God's house.
context.canonical: Marks the transition from Book I's largely Davidic collection to Book II's broader collection including the Korahite psalms, and its refrain of the soul preaching hope to itself has become a touchstone for Christian devotional reflection on honest lament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model honest lament that neither denies real despair nor surrenders to it, but actively preaches hope to one's own soul by remembering God's past faithfulness and fixing hope on future praise.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the psalmist's particular exile and longing for the temple; it prescribes the practice of addressing one's own soul directly with truth about God as the pattern for facing downcast despair in any circumstance.

debates.question: Does 'deep calls to deep' in verse 7 describe overwhelming affliction, or a deeper spiritual experience of God's presence within the affliction?

views.label: The phrase primarily describes the overwhelming, crushing weight of the psalmist's affliction, using the roar of cataracts and crashing waves as a picture of trouble piling upon trouble
views.case: The following clause, 'all Your breakers and waves have rolled over me,' uses drowning imagery consistently applied elsewhere in the Psalms to overwhelming distress, suggesting the phrase describes affliction's depth, not primarily divine communion.

views.label: The phrase suggests a mysterious depth calling to depth between the psalmist's own deep need and God's deep, sovereign purposes, God's own waters and waterfalls being invoked even in the crushing experience, God active even within the affliction
views.case: The verse explicitly names 'Your waterfalls' and 'Your breakers and waves,' attributing the overwhelming experience directly to God's own sovereign hand rather than merely impersonal circumstance, suggesting even the crushing depth is somehow God's own deep calling to the psalmist's deep need.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalmist experiences overwhelming affliction that he attributes ultimately to God's sovereign hand rather than blind chance, which is precisely why he can still address his soul with hope, the same God who sends the waves is the God to whom he prays by night and day.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist's longing is arag, intense panting, elsewhere describing animals crying out in drought, and his soul is shachach, bowed down and sunk low, yet he directly addresses that downcast soul rather than simply submitting to its despair, commanding it three times across this psalm and Psalm 43 to hope in God.

typology.type: The soul deeply downcast and crushed with sorrow, verses 5-6, 11
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own soul deeply grieved to the point of death in Gethsemane, Matthew 26:38.

typology.type: The taunt 'Where is your God?', verses 3, 10
typology.fulfillment: The nearly identical mockery hurled at Jesus on the cross, 'He trusts in God; let God rescue Him now,' Matthew 27:43.

study.christ: The mockery this psalmist endures, 'Where is your God?', anticipates the almost identical taunt hurled at Jesus on the cross, 'He trusts in God; let God rescue Him now if He wants Him,' Matthew 27:43. Christ's own soul was 'deeply grieved, to the point of death,' Matthew 26:38, entering fully into this psalm's downcast anguish, yet He is also the living water this psalmist's soul thirsts for, later offering Himself, 'If anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink,' John 7:37, so that the hope this psalm refuses to abandon finds its full answer in Him.
study.god: God is revealed as the living water for which the human soul is designed to thirst, present in overwhelming waves as much as in quiet waters, decreeing steadfast love by day and giving a song even in the darkest night.

crossReferences.note: Mockers taunt Jesus on the cross with words strikingly similar to this psalm's 'Where is your God?'.
Jesus's own soul is deeply grieved, even to death, echoing this psalm's downcast soul.
Jesus offers Himself as living water to all who thirst, answering this psalm's central longing directly.

study.oneStory: The psalmist's thirsting, downcast soul, preaching hope to itself amid taunts asking 'Where is your God?', anticipates Christ, who entered this very anguish and endured nearly identical mockery on the cross, yet who is Himself the living water for which every soul longs and the sure ground of the hope this psalm refuses to relinquish.

sermon.title: Prayer of Thirst
sermon.why: Keller preaches on Psalm 42:1-43:5 as one continuous prayer, showing how the psalmist's practice of talking to his own soul rather than merely listening to it models honest, hope-filled lament for anyone walking through spiritual darkness or distance from God.

quiz.q: What image does the psalmist use in verse 1 to describe his soul's longing for God?

options.text: A deer panting intensely for streams of water.
options.note: The Hebrew arag describes the desperate panting of a thirst-stricken animal, pictured elsewhere only in drought conditions, Joel 1:20.

options.text: A bird flying freely through the sky.
options.note: The text specifically uses the image of a panting deer thirsting for water, not a flying bird.

options.text: A tree planted firmly by streams of water.
options.note: That image appears in Psalm 1:3; this psalm's image is a panting, thirsty deer, emphasizing desperate longing rather than stability.

options.text: A shepherd searching for lost sheep.
options.note: The psalm's image is of the soul itself thirsting like a deer, not a shepherd searching for sheep.

quiz.q: What taunt do the psalmist's enemies repeat, according to verses 3 and 10?

options.text: "Where is your God?"
options.note: This exact taunt, repeated twice, anticipates the strikingly similar mockery hurled at Jesus on the cross in Matthew 27:43.

options.text: "You will never see the temple again."
options.note: While related to his separation from the temple, the explicit repeated taunt quoted in the text is 'Where is your God?'

options.text: "Your God has blessed you too much."
options.note: The taunt mocks the apparent absence of God's help, not excessive blessing.

options.text: "Your enemies will show you mercy."
options.note: The taunt is hostile mockery about God's apparent absence, not a promise of mercy from enemies.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist do in response to his own downcast, despairing feelings, verses 5 and 11?

options.text: He directly addresses his own soul, questioning its despair and commanding it to hope in God.
options.note: This refrain, repeated at the end of each movement, models actively preaching hope to oneself rather than simply obeying one's feelings.

options.text: He simply agrees with his despair and gives up hope entirely.
options.note: The refrain explicitly commands the soul to hope in God rather than surrendering to despair.

options.text: He blames God directly for abandoning him with no further reflection.
options.note: While he does ask 'Why have You forgotten me?' in verse 9, the refrain moves beyond blame to a deliberate command to hope.

options.text: He ignores his feelings entirely and pretends to feel nothing.
options.note: The psalmist openly names his downcast feelings rather than denying them; he then addresses and challenges them, not ignores them.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's central taunt anticipate an event in the Gospels?

options.text: It anticipates the mockery hurled at Jesus on the cross, 'He trusts in God; let God rescue Him now,' Matthew 27:43.
options.note: The wording and mocking intent closely parallel this psalm's repeated taunt, 'Where is your God?'

options.text: It anticipates the disciples' confession of faith at Caesarea Philippi.
options.note: That confession, Matthew 16:16, is an affirmation of faith, not a taunt questioning God's presence.

options.text: It anticipates the angel's announcement at the empty tomb.
options.note: The angel's announcement is a proclamation of resurrection, not a hostile taunt about God's absence.

options.text: It anticipates the crowd's shouts of Hosanna at the triumphal entry.
options.note: Those shouts are praise and welcome, the opposite tone of the mocking taunt this psalm anticipates.

quiz.q: What memory does the psalmist recall in verse 4 that deepens his present sorrow?

options.text: Walking with the multitude, leading the procession to the house of God with shouts of joy and praise.
options.note: Remembering past festival worship intensifies his present grief at being separated from God's house.

options.text: A time of military victory over his enemies.
options.note: The specific memory recalled is of joyful temple worship and procession, not military victory.

options.text: A conversation with the enemies who now taunt him.
options.note: The memory is of joyful worship with the multitude, not any conversation with his current enemies.

options.text: His original calling as a shepherd.
options.note: The remembered scene is specifically temple worship and festival procession, not a shepherding memory.

questions: The psalmist describes his longing for God as a deer desperately panting for water. Would you describe your own current longing for God's presence as desperate thirst, or has it grown more casual? What might rekindle that thirst?

The psalmist's enemies taunt him with 'Where is your God?' during his hardest season. Have you faced a similar taunt, from others or from your own doubting heart, during a time when God's presence felt hidden?

Rather than simply obeying his despair, the psalmist directly addresses his own soul and commands it to hope. What would it look like this week to actively preach truth to your own downcast soul rather than just listening to how you feel?

The psalmist remembers past seasons of joyful worship even while presently grieving. How can remembering specific past experiences of God's nearness help sustain hope during a present season of spiritual dryness or distance?

This psalm's taunt anticipates the mockery Jesus endured on the cross. How does knowing that Christ Himself entered this exact kind of anguish and mockery change how you bring your own downcast soul to Him?

translationNotes.note: Pants renders arag, describing the desperate, physical panting of a thirst-stricken animal, used elsewhere only in Joel 1:20 of creatures crying out in drought.
Downcast renders shachach, bowed down or sunk low; the psalmist directly addresses this state in his own soul rather than simply submitting to it, a refrain repeated in verses 5, 11, and 43:5.
Deep calls to deep uses the roar of cataracts and crashing waves as an image of overwhelming affliction, while still naming the waters and waves as God's own, 'Your waterfalls,' 'Your breakers.'

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 42:1-11. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch confirming Tim Keller's message 'Prayer of Thirst' on Psalm 42:1-43:5 at podcast.gospelinlife.com.
